ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic fried rice class; no specific Singapore-style egg fried rice with pork chop entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers fried rice as a category; no sub-code specific to egg fried rice with pork chop exists in the public FoodEx2 browser.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard canteen plate of egg fried rice with pork chop weighs approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 250 g of egg fried rice and a 100 g boneless pork chop portion.

nutrient_source.title: Egg fried rice with pork chop — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo record for 'Egg fried rice with pork chop' (cr_id F05100051). All seven reported nutrients are used as-is. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy analogous composite (fried rice with pork) because HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice, fried rice analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for egg fried rice with pork chop. GI of 75 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice (jasmine/long-grain, cooked), which reports GI ~72–76. A midpoint of 75 is used as the analogous estimate for this dish, which is predominantly white rice. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (75 × 71.4) / 100 ≈ 53.5, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 20.39 × 3.5 = 71.4 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 393 mg per 100 g, primarily from soy sauce and added salt used in stir-frying, with minimal potassium or dietary fibre to offset the sodium load.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 200 g per meal, request reduced soy sauce and no added salt, and choose a grilled rather than fried pork chop to reduce both sodium and saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ~75) with 20.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g and only 0.5 g dietary fibre, resulting in rapid post-prandial glucose elevation and a high glycemic load of approximately 53.5 per standard serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict portion size to 150–200 g, request brown rice substitution to lower the glycemic index, and pair the dish with non-starchy vegetables to slow carbohydrate absorption and blunt the post-meal glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.3 g per 100 g) from pork and cooking oil, combined with high sodium (393 mg per 100 g) and negligible dietary fibre (0.5 g per 100 g), presents a composite cardiovascular risk profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, choose a lean grilled pork chop, request reduced oil and soy sauce, and supplement the meal with a side of vegetables to improve the overall dietary fibre and micronutrient balance.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Egg fried rice with pork chop is a popular Singaporean Chinese canteen and hawker dish consisting of wok-stir-fried white rice tossed with beaten egg, soy sauce, sesame oil, and spring onion, served alongside a grilled or pan-fried boneless pork chop that is typically marinated in soy sauce, garlic, and five-spice powder. The rice is cooked over high heat in a wok to achieve a lightly charred, fragrant texture, while the pork chop contributes a savoury, slightly caramelised exterior with a tender interior. Per 100 g, the dish provides 200 kcal, 12.0 g protein, 7.8 g fat, and 20.4 g carbohydrate, with 393 mg sodium and only 0.5 g dietary fibre. The high sodium content reflects the liberal use of soy sauce in both the rice and the pork marinade. The white rice base carries a high glycemic index of approximately 75, making this dish a high-glycemic-load meal that is unsuitable for frequent consumption by individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension. The pork chop contributes saturated fat and arachidonic acid (omega-6), which is relevant for individuals with inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. This dish is a staple in Singapore school canteens and coffee shops (kopitiams), commonly consumed at lunch. It is not halal due to the pork content.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05100051), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. The exact dish is present in the HPB database with seven nutrient fields reported at 0.95 confidence. Saturated fat is estimated from a USDA SR Legacy analogous composite and is clearly documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white rice, which is the dominant carbohydrate component.
notes: sugar_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB for this dish; this is a confirmed zero per the approved source (HPB Foodo, F05100051) and is plausible given the savoury preparation with no added sugar. The following fields are set to -1 because HPB Foodo does not report these nutrients for this composite dish entry, and no secondary approved source (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy) provides a sufficiently close composite match to justify imputation without risk of material error: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g — detailed fatty acid fractions not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry and no approved composite analogue with sufficient specificity was found in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026-04-16; potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg — mineral and cholesterol data not reported by HPB Foodo for this composite dish; vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg — vitamin micronutrient data not reported by HPB Foodo for this composite dish; phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg — trace mineral data not reported by HPB Foodo for this composite dish; water_g — moisture content not reported by HPB Foodo for this composite dish. All -1 values reflect genuine data absence in the primary approved source (HPB Foodo) and the absence of a sufficiently close approved secondary source match, not a processing omission. saturated_fat_g of 2.3 g per 100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 'Rice, fried, Chinese restaurant' adjusted for pork chop contribution; this is an approximation and should be replaced with a direct HPB measurement if one becomes available. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate based on white rice (jasmine/long-grain) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in any approved source as of 2026-04-16.
